content.text:
**Analysis:**
- Holding A3o in SB (small blind), facing BB's 2-chip blind — only 1 to call
- Opponent: VPIP 59%, PFR 13% — loose-passive, calls wide but rarely raises
- Folds to flop c-bets 58% (7/12) and river bets 75% (3/4)
- Showdown wins 89% — when they reach showdown they have it, but they fold to pressure often

**Decision:** A3o is a solid raise from the button/SB. Villain is loose-passive so they'll call, but our c-bet equity is strong (58% fold rate). Raising builds a pot we can win with position and continuation bets. Standard raise to ~3 BB.

Villain's donk bet could represent a J, or a draw, or a weak hand trying to take it down. 

Given their loose-passive tendencies, they probably have something concrete like a J or a pocket pair. Their high showdown win rate means when they stick around, they're holding strong hands, so I'm likely behind with just ace-high. I'm weighing whether to fold, call to see the turn with my two overcards and decent pot odds, or raise as a semi-bluff. Raising risks them calling with a J when I'm already behind, so calling seems more prudent to evaluate what comes on the turn.

The pot odds work out to needing about 22% equity, and AK has reasonable equity against their likely range even if it doesn't improve, so I'm calling.

Villain bet 22 into 50, so I need about 30% equity to call. My 10 outs give me roughly 22% on the river - not quite there. But villain only has 88 left after this bet, so if I call the 22, they'd be shoving the remaining 66 on the river. Looking at their betting pattern on this J-3-7-T board, they probably have a jack, ten, or a pocket pair, though KQ would give them the straight already.

The real play here is the implied odds. If I hit an ace, king, or queen on the river, I can extract villain's remaining 66 chips from a pot that's already 94. That's 160 total - much better than the immediate 30% I need. I'm calling and hoping to catch Broadway.

content.text:

- **Implied odds:** Villain only has 66 chips behind after this bet. If I hit an A, K, or Q on the river I can likely stack them — implied value is massive relative to the 22 call
- **Q specifically gives Broadway (the nuts)** — extremely strong if it hits

The implied odds justify the call given villain's short remaining stack.

Board: 8h-6h-Tc-6c-8d. I have KsTd = tens and eights two pair... wait, the board has Tc, 6c, 6c, 8d. Board is 8h-6h-Tc-6c-8d. 

My hand: Ks-Td with board 8h-6h-Tc-6c-8d.
Best 5-card hand: Tc-Td-8h-8d-Ks = two pair, tens and eights with K kicker.

But the board itself has two pair: 8-8-6-6-T. My best hand uses the board: T-T-8-8-K. 

That's two pair tens and eights. Villain's been calling through flop and turn, and they're a strong showdown player with an 87% win rate, which makes river betting risky. They rarely bet the river themselves, but the double-paired board means they could easily have a full house with a 6 or 8. My two pair is solid but not dominant here, and their fold-to-river-bet rate of 64% suggests they'll call down fairly often.

The math is tricky—betting for value and getting called is probably losing given their showdown dominance, but the fold equity from that 64% fold rate makes a semi-bluff worthwhile. I'll bet around 70 into the 128 pot, accepting that if they call, they likely have me beat, but the fold equity makes it profitable overall.

- VPIP 82%, PFR 22% - very loose passive

content.text:
**Analysis:**
- Villain is very loose-passive (VPIP 82%, PFR 22%) — plays a ton of hands, rarely aggressive
- Folds to flop c-bets **59%** (17/29) — profitable c-betting spot
- Folds to river bets **67%** (10/15) — even better river fold rate
- Only 3-bets ~4% of the time — minimal preflop re-raise risk
- Strong showdown caller (88% win rate) — don't bluff to showdown

**Decision:** 9h6s is marginal but the pot odds are excellent (4:1 on a call), and against a loose-passive villain who folds to c-bets 59%+ of the time, playing in position and firing c-bets postflop is clearly +EV. Raising is risky given villain's loose calling range; calling and playing exploitatively postflop is the right line.

- VPIP 91%, PFR 26% - very loose passive

content.text:
**Analysis:**
- Villain is very loose-passive (VPIP 91%, PFR 26%) and folds to flop c-bets 61% of the time.
- Villain rarely 3-bets (~3.4% of hands), so a preflop raise is low-risk.
- Taking the betting lead with 7h-9s gives me a profitable c-bet opportunity on most flops.
- Villain's showdown win rate (89%) means I should avoid bluffing to showdown — but with initiative, I can win without showdown.

**Decision:** Raise to take initiative and set up profitable c-bets.

content.thinking:
Turn is 6s. Board: Ah Jc Ks 6s. I have Q9h - still have the gutshot to broadway (need T). One card left. ~8.5% to hit. Pot odds: 18/(54+18) = 25%. 8.5% equity doesn't justify a call purely. 

But implied odds: if I hit the T on the river, villain has ~198 stack, pot will be 90, and villain might call a big bet. If villain calls all-in on river, I'd win ~198 more. So total I'd win ~90+198=288 if I hit vs losing 18 now. 

The pot odds require 25% equity, but I'm only at 8.5%. However, when I factor in the expected value with villain stacking off—8.5% chance of winning 288 minus the 91.5% chance of losing 18—that works out to roughly +8 in my favor. Villain probably won't always stack off, but given how passive they play, the implied odds make this a reasonable call.
